title: stalld’s BPF Backend: Breaking Free from debugfs
summary: For years, stalld has been a critical tool for maintaining stability in real-time and CPU-isolated Linux environments. Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application. The original mechanism for this detection was straightforward but crude...

sections.overview: For years, stalld has been a critical tool for maintaining stability in real-time and CPU-isolated Linux environments. Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application. The original mechanism for this detection was straightforward but crude: stalld would periodically read and parse the text output of /sys/kernel/debug/sched/debug .
sections.technical_breakdown:
Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application.

When a user-space application is spinning at 100% on an isolated core, these kernel tasks can get "stuck" in the CPU's runqueue, waiting indefinitely for the user-space task to yield.

If a kernel thread was enqueued and subsequently dequeued between two stalld polls, its starvation event would be completely missed.

This tracepoint allows for clean-up.
 sched_migrate_task : Tasks can be moved between CPU runqueues.

In high-performance computing (HPC) and network function virtualization (NFV) workloads, particularly those using frameworks like DPDK, it's common practice to dedicate one or more CPU cores to a single, high-priority application.
sections.impact:
Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application. When a user-space application is spinning at 100% on an isolated core, these kernel tasks can get "stuck" in the CPU's runqueue, waiting indefinitely for the user-space task to yield. Instead of asking the kernel for a giant text dump and parsing it in user space, we can now place small, highly efficient BPF programs at strategic points in the kernel's scheduler code. The stalld user-space daemon simply reads from this structured map.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: networking.
sections.resolution: Each new kernel version risked breaking stalld until we manually patched it.
sections.lessons_learned:
Portability with BPF CO-RE : Kernel data structures change between versions — this is the same fundamental challenge we faced with debugfs , but BPF CO-RE provides far better tooling to handle it.

When a task is switched in (begins executing), we know it's no longer waiting, so we remove it from our tracking map.
 sched_process_exit : When a task exits, stalld must remove any associated tracking data from our maps to prevent stale entries.
sections.trends: Large-scale outages remain the forcing function for resilient architecture, communication, and automation investments.

Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application.
